doc.title: Issue common stock
doc.event: Nola Corp. sells 10,000 shares of its common stock at $15 per share. Prepare the issuance entry.

variants.label: $1 par sold at $15 — par to the stock account, the rest to PIC

lines.why: Proceeds are proceeds — shares × issue price, regardless of par.
lines.trap: Par has nothing to do with how much cash comes in. It only controls the split below.

lines.label: 10,000 × $1 par
lines.why: The stock account is credited for PAR ONLY — a legal-capital relic, not a value measure.
lines.trap: Crediting Common Stock for the full $150,000 is the #1 issuance error. Par × shares, nothing more.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
lines.label: the other $14/share
lines.why: Everything investors paid above par lands in additional paid-in capital — still contributed capital, just labeled honestly.
lines.trap: This is NOT a gain. A company never earns income by issuing its own stock.

variants.label: No-par with a $2 stated value — stated value plays the par role

entries.caption: Issue 10,000 no-par shares, $2 stated value, at $15

lines.trap: Stated value changes the split, never the cash.

lines.label: 10,000 × $2 stated
lines.why: Stated value is board-declared par-by-another-name; the stock account takes stated × shares.
lines.trap: Treat stated value exactly like par mechanically — students who learn them as different rules make double the errors.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Stated Value
lines.label: the excess
lines.trap: Match the account NAME to the regime: 'in Excess of Stated Value' here, 'in Excess of Par' there. Graders check.

variants.label: True no-par — the whole proceeds go to the stock account

lines.trap: Two-line entry — no excess account exists when there's no par or stated value to be in excess OF.

lines.label: full proceeds
lines.why: With no par and no stated value, the entire $150,000 is legal capital in the stock account.
lines.trap: Inventing a PIC line here is the mirror-image error of forgetting it in the par case. Read the share type first, then pick the pattern.

memorize.body: Common Stock credit = shares × par (or stated value). PIC in Excess = proceeds − that. True no-par: Common Stock takes it all.
Par is a legal floor from another century, not a price. A $1-par share selling at $15 is completely normal — the $14 gap is the whole reason PIC accounts exist.
Issuance costs (underwriting, legal) DEBIT paid-in capital — they reduce the proceeds recorded, they are never expensed as a period cost.

questions.prompt: 10,000 shares of $1-par common are issued at $15. What is credited to the Common Stock account?

distractors.feedback: That's the CASH — the full proceeds. Common Stock takes only par × shares ($10,000); the other $140,000 belongs in PIC in Excess of Par.
You've swapped the two credits — $140,000 is the PIC piece. The stock account gets the small number: par × shares.

accountBank.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
Paid-in Capital in Excess of Stated Value

build.scaffold: Cash first (shares × price — par is irrelevant to it). Then ask: par, stated value, or neither? Par/stated × shares to the stock account, remainder to the matching PIC account, and true no-par means no remainder at all.
